mnemonics.title: PTHREAD FUNCTIONS MEMORY AID
mnemonics.content: CREATE-JOIN-LOCK-UNLOCK-CONDITION-WAIT-SIGNAL-BROADCAST: C-J-L-U-C-W-S-B covers the main pthread functions: pthread_create, pthread_join, pthread_mutex_lock, pthread_mutex_unlock, pthread_cond_wait, pthread_cond_signal, pthread_cond_broadcast

mnemonics.title: MUTEX RULES
mnemonics.content: LOCK ONE: Always lock before accessing shared data, Unlock after, Nesting can cause deadlock, Never forget to unlock, Execute in critical section only, Handle errors properly

mnemonics.title: THREAD CREATION STEPS
mnemonics.content: CREATE A THREAD: C-reate thread ID variable, R-eady attributes (or NULL for defaults), E-nter start routine, A-llocate argument, T-hread create call

acronyms.expansion: POSIX Threads - Portable Operating System Interface threading standard
acronyms.usage: Used for creating portable multi-threaded applications in C/C++ on UNIX systems

acronyms.expansion: Thread-Specific Data - Data private to each thread
acronyms.usage: Used when each thread needs its own copy of global or static variables

acronyms.expansion: Portable Operating System Interface [X] - International standard for OS compatibility
acronyms.usage: Defines the Pthreads API used across different UNIX-like operating systems

acronyms.expansion: Simultaneous Processing Of Parallel Applications with Multiple Threads
acronyms.usage: Mnemonic for remembering thread creation allows parallel execution

acronyms.expansion: Race And Concurrent Execution - condition when threads compete for resources
acronyms.usage: Describes the problem that synchronization primitives like mutexes prevent

visualTips.visualization: Draw a main thread (box) with an arrow pointing to a new thread box being created, with pthread_create() as the connector label. The new thread shows execution starting from a function.
Think of a mutex as a single key to a room. Only one person (thread) can hold the key (lock) at a time. Others must wait outside until the key is returned (unlock).

Draw two boxes (producers) putting items into a queue (buffer), and two boxes (consumers) taking items. Condition variables act like notification bells - producers ring bell when buffer has space, consumers ring when buffer has items.
Use a traffic light analogy: RED (blocked/waiting), YELLOW (ready to run), GREEN (running). Threads move between these states based on scheduler decisions.

analogies.analogy: Think of a process as a factory and threads as workers in that factory. All workers share the same factory resources (memory, files) but can perform different tasks simultaneously.
A mutex is like a bathroom key in a small office. Only one person can hold the key and use the bathroom at a time. Others must wait until the key is returned.
A semaphore with count N is like a parking lot with N spaces. Cars (threads) can enter only if spaces are available. When full, cars must wait outside.
A condition variable is like a doorbell. A thread waiting for data rings the doorbell (waits). When another thread adds data, it rings the doorbell (signals) to wake up the waiting thread.

If processes are houses, threads are people living in the same house. They share the house (memory) but each person can do different things. Creating a new person (thread) is much faster than building a new house (process).
